section_text_parts: If you are planning to use digital signature algorithms like RSA or ECDSA, you should install the cryptography library dependency pyjwt[crypto] .
You can read more about it in the PyJWT Installation docs .
tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt::section-00.section_text:
If you are planning to use digital signature algorithms like RSA or ECDSA, you should install the cryptography library dependency pyjwt[crypto] .

You can read more about it in the PyJWT Installation docs .

section_text_parts: With pwdlib , you could even configure it to be able to read passwords created by Django , a Flask security plug-in or many others.
So, you would be able to, for example, share the same data from a Django application in a database with a FastAPI application. Or gradually migrate a Django application using the same database.
And your users would be able to login from your Django app or from your FastAPI app, at the same time.
tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt::section-00.section_text:
With pwdlib , you could even configure it to be able to read passwords created by Django , a Flask security plug-in or many others.

So, you would be able to, for example, share the same data from a Django application in a database with a FastAPI application. Or gradually migrate a Django application using the same database.

And your users would be able to login from your Django app or from your FastAPI app, at the same time.

section_text_parts: pwdlib also supports the bcrypt hashing algorithm but does not include legacy algorithms - for working with outdated hashes, it is recommended to use the passlib library.
For example, you could use it to read and verify passwords generated by another system (like Django) but hash any new passwords with a different algorithm like Argon2 or Bcrypt.
And be compatible with all of them at the same time.
tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt::section-00.section_text:
pwdlib also supports the bcrypt hashing algorithm but does not include legacy algorithms - for working with outdated hashes, it is recommended to use the passlib library.

For example, you could use it to read and verify passwords generated by another system (like Django) but hash any new passwords with a different algorithm like Argon2 or Bcrypt.

And be compatible with all of them at the same time.

section_text_parts: If you check the new (fake) database fake_users_db , you will see how the hashed password looks like now: "$argon2id$v=19$m=65536,t=3,p=4$wagCPXjifgvUFBzq4hqe3w$CYaIb8sB+wtD+Vu/P4uod1+Qof8h+1g7bbDlBID48Rc" .
tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt::section-00.section_text: If you check the new (fake) database fake_users_db , you will see how the hashed password looks like now: "$argon2id$v=19$m=65536,t=3,p=4$wagCPXjifgvUFBzq4hqe3w$CYaIb8sB+wtD+Vu/P4uod1+Qof8h+1g7bbDlBID48Rc" .

section_text_parts: Notice that nowhere in the code is the plaintext password " secret ", we only have the hashed version.
tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt::section-00.section_text: Notice that nowhere in the code is the plaintext password " secret ", we only have the hashed version.

section_text_parts: Notice the header Authorization , with a value that starts with Bearer .
tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt::section-00.section_text: Notice the header Authorization , with a value that starts with Bearer .
